source_title: Teaching Data Journalism in New Zealand
author: Grant Hannis

extracts.extract: I ask the students to suggest stories that might arise from the data. At first, the students struggle with this and often do not suggest ideas directly related to the data. Instead, they suggest more conventional types of journalism. I encourage the students to think directly about the data: what stories can be found there?
extracts.notes: Highlights a common novice struggle: defaulting to conventional journalism frames rather than letting data suggest stories
extracts.relevance_rationale: Teaching students to find stories directly in data rather than defaulting to conventional journalism frames is an enduring pedagogical challenge

extracts.extract: I ask the students whether we need to do anything to make the data more manageable. The students are somewhat intimidated by the data's status as official statistics. Gradually I steer the conversation to the need to rationalize some of the data to make it easier to use -- for instance, removing subtotals and summing the data for China and Hong Kong. I then clean up the data in Excel and sort it from highest to lowest.
extracts.notes: Students intimidated by official data; cleaning framed as making data 'manageable' rather than fixing errors
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual data cleaning in Excel endures as pedagogy but should now also teach LLM-assisted cleaning approaches

extracts.extract: I ask the students how we can visually depict the data. I remind the students that data journalism usually has visually engaging and interactive graphics. I introduce the students to Datawrapper, a free online software tool that can produce visual displays of data with simple interactivity. I take the students through the process of inputting the data into a world map using a Datawrapper template.
extracts.notes: Datawrapper chosen for accessibility and simplicity
extracts.relevance_rationale: Datawrapper remains relevant but LLMs can now generate visualizations from natural language, expanding the tool landscape for teaching

extracts.extract: Drawing on an earlier discussion on graphing data, I remind the students of the need for good statistical practice. The world map's title should clearly say what the chart is displaying and should state the source of the data.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Clear titles, source attribution, and good statistical practice in visualizations are enduring presentation standards

extracts.extract: To help the students grasp what data journalism is, I ask them to visit an excellent New Zealand data journalism site. I use a New Zealand site because it has direct applicability to the students, covering topics they identify with and showing them that New Zealand journalists do data journalism. Typically, this piques the students' interest.
extracts.notes: Pedagogical principle: use locally relevant published data journalism to motivate and orient students
extracts.relevance_rationale: Using locally relevant published examples to motivate students is an enduring pedagogical principle

extracts.extract: I ask them to suggest some possible data journalism topics, in particular thinking of how topics they have written articles about themselves could be developed as pieces of data journalism. I also ask them to suggest sources of data for such articles.
extracts.notes: Bridges conventional reporting experience to data journalism by reframing existing beats
extracts.relevance_rationale: Brainstorming DJ topics from existing beats and identifying data sources is an enduring story ideation practice

extracts.extract: To ensure the students understand how to undertake the data visualization, each student creates his/her own world map of the data. I inspect each map to make sure it has been done correctly.
extracts.notes: Verification through reproduction: students must individually replicate the demonstrated workflow
extracts.relevance_rationale: Hands-on replication of demonstrated workflows as assessment is an enduring pedagogical method

extracts.extract: To teach journalism students data journalism and statistics generally, the lecturer must have a strong relevant background. I have a PhD in economics and before coming to Massey worked for many years as a financial journalist. I had to learn new skills to design and teach the data journalism module. Partly, I taught myself, including using the very helpful tutorials on the Datawrapper website.
extracts.notes: Argues data journalism instruction requires both quantitative and journalistic expertise in the instructor
extracts.relevance_rationale: Instructor technical competence requirements persist but the specific skills needed now include LLM literacy alongside quantitative and journalistic expertise

extracts.extract: Stats NZ release most of their information as dense Excel spreadsheets. Since this module, I have gone through several spreadsheets while writing a story and been able to recognise and translate this information to readers.
extracts.notes: Student testimony; describes workflow of obtaining government Excel files and interpreting them for readers
extracts.relevance_rationale: Working with government Excel spreadsheets endures but LLMs can now assist with interpreting and translating dense official data

extracts.extract: I would like to take the students through more of the functionality of Excel, so they become more conversant in the basic tasks of cleaning up and manipulating data. I would also like to require the students to each produce their own piece of data journalism, in the form of an article and an interactive chart.
extracts.notes: Aspirational; argues cleaning and manipulation need more instructional time alongside a production-based assessment
extracts.relevance_rationale: Teaching Excel functionality endures but curriculum should now include LLM-assisted data manipulation alongside traditional spreadsheet skills

extracts.extract: I have frequently encountered journalism students' aversion to mathematics. Attendance was so poor at my early classes that I felt obliged to introduce a compulsory test to encourage the students to attend. As well as this stick, I have recently been able to introduce a carrot -- a NZ business journalism prize for the best business journalism student on the course.
extracts.notes: Institutional challenge: maths aversion requires both compulsory assessment and positive incentives to overcome
extracts.relevance_rationale: Student maths aversion and the need for both compulsory assessment and positive incentives are enduring pedagogical challenges
